symbols: blue cross form — the instrument of salvation rendered in pure color, stripped of narrative to reveal its essential spiritual meaning
black circle — darkness, void, or death positioned above the cross, possibly evoking the eclipse at Calvary or the cosmic darkness Christ overcame
green diagonal bar — life, growth, and renewal emerging from the base of the cross, the regenerative power of the resurrection
purple diagonal bar — royalty, penitence, and the Passion, the liturgical color of suffering and kingly authority
orange field — warmth, energy, and the fiery presence of the Spirit enveloping the symbol of redemption
description: A contemporary abstract composition on a textured orange field. A bold blue cross dominates the center, formed by two intersecting rectangular bars. A solid black circle sits in the upper left area above the crossbar. Below the intersection of the cross, a green diagonal bar extends downward to the left and a purple diagonal bar extends downward to the right, creating a dynamic angular form beneath the cross. The surface shows visible brushwork texture throughout the orange background. The geometric forms have clean, hard edges and saturated primary and secondary colors.
theological_description: Antonakos's abstract treatment of the cross strips the central Christian symbol to its geometric essence, inviting contemplation unconditioned by figurative narrative. The cross has been the supreme symbol of Christianity since the earliest centuries — what was an instrument of Roman execution became, through Christ's death and resurrection, the sign of salvation, victory over death, and the paradox of divine power made manifest in weakness. By rendering it in pure blue against a vibrant orange field, Antonakos evokes the tension between heaven (blue) and earth/fire (orange). The black circle may recall the darkness that fell over the land at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:45), while the green and purple diagonals emerging below suggest the dual nature of the cross's aftermath: new life (green, resurrection) and royal suffering (purple, Passion). The abstract idiom serves a theological purpose: it universalizes the cross beyond any single historical moment, presenting it as an eternal sign that transcends time and culture. As Paul wrote, 'The message of the cross is foolishness to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God' (1 Corinthians 1:18).

scripture_refs.reason: The message of the cross as the power of God — the theological core of any artistic meditation on the cross symbol
Paul's declaration that he boasts only in the cross of Christ — the cross as the definitive Christian symbol
Darkness over the land from the sixth to ninth hour during the crucifixion, possibly evoked by the black circle
Christ disarming the powers by the cross, triumphing over them — the cross as instrument of cosmic victory
Obedient unto death, even death on a cross — the kenotic theology the cross universally signifies
